event: Early modern humans inhabit the region, leaving some of Europe's oldest Homo sapiens remains in the Peștera cu Oase cave.
The Neolithic Cucuteni-Trypillia culture flourishes, producing sophisticated painted pottery and large settlements.
Greek colonists found trading colonies such as Histria on the Black Sea coast of Dobruja.
The Persian king Darius I campaigns against the Getae, the earliest recorded Dacian-related people in the region.
King Burebista unites the Dacian and Getic tribes into a powerful kingdom centered in the Carpathians.
Emperor Trajan defeats King Decebalus, conquering Dacia and incorporating it as a Roman province.
Emperor Aurelian withdraws Roman administration and legions from Dacia under barbarian pressure.
The Huns drive Gothic and other peoples across the Danube, beginning waves of migrations through the region.
Early Romanian voivodeships and cnezates emerge as the Romanian (Vlach) ethnogenesis takes shape north of the Danube.
The Mongol invasion devastates the lands of Transylvania, Moldavia, and Wallachia.
Basarab I defeats the Hungarians at Posada, securing the independence of the principality of Wallachia.
Bogdan I founds an independent principality of Moldavia, breaking from Hungarian suzerainty.
Stephen the Great becomes Prince of Moldavia, leading decades of resistance against the Ottomans.
Vlad the Impaler (Vlad Țepeș), prince of Wallachia, dies after fierce campaigns against the Ottomans.
Transylvania becomes an autonomous principality under Ottoman suzerainty after the fall of Hungary.
Michael the Brave briefly unites Wallachia, Moldavia, and Transylvania under a single rule.
The Treaty of Karlowitz transfers Transylvania from Ottoman to Habsburg control.
Russia annexes Bessarabia, the eastern half of Moldavia, under the Treaty of Bucharest.
Tudor Vladimirescu leads a major uprising in Wallachia against Phanariot and Ottoman rule.
Revolutions sweep Wallachia, Moldavia, and Transylvania, advancing liberal and national ideals.
Alexandru Ioan Cuza is elected prince of both Moldavia and Wallachia, uniting the two principalities.
The united principalities formally adopt the name Romania with Bucharest as capital.
Carol I of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen becomes ruling prince and a new liberal constitution is adopted.
Romania declares independence from the Ottoman Empire during the Russo-Turkish War.
The Treaty of Berlin recognizes Romanian independence and grants Northern Dobruja to Romania.
Romania is proclaimed a kingdom and Carol I is crowned its first king.
Romania gains Southern Dobruja from Bulgaria after participating in the Second Balkan War.
Romania enters World War I on the Allied side, suffering heavy losses and occupation of much of the country.
Bessarabia, Bukovina, and Transylvania unite with Romania, creating Greater Romania.
A new constitution consolidates the enlarged Greater Romania as a centralized constitutional monarchy.
King Carol II establishes a royal dictatorship, suspending the parliamentary constitution.
Romania loses Bessarabia, Northern Bukovina, Northern Transylvania, and Southern Dobruja, and Ion Antonescu takes power.
Romania joins the Axis and enters World War II alongside Nazi Germany against the Soviet Union.
King Michael I leads a coup ousting Antonescu, and Romania switches to the Allied side.
The communists force King Michael I to abdicate, proclaiming the Romanian People's Republic.
Nicolae Ceaușescu becomes leader of the Communist Party, beginning his long authoritarian rule.
Ceaușescu condemns the Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia, boosting his international standing.
A devastating earthquake strikes Bucharest and the region, killing over 1,500 people.
A revolution overthrows and executes Ceaușescu, ending communist rule in Romania.
A new democratic constitution is adopted, establishing Romania as a semi-presidential republic.
Romania completes major economic reforms and privatization as it prepares for Euro-Atlantic integration.
Romania joins the NATO alliance.
Romania becomes a member of the European Union.
Klaus Iohannis is elected president, the first ethnic German to hold the office.
Romania's annulled presidential election triggers a major political crisis amid allegations of foreign interference.
